coredata.dc:title: Mild near-infrared laser-triggered photo-immunotherapy potentiates immune checkpoint blockade via an all-in-one theranostic nanoplatform

coredata.dc:description: One of the primary challenges for immune checkpoint blockade (ICB)-based therapy is the limited infiltration of T lymphocytes (T cells) into tumors, often referred to as immunologically “cold” tumors. A promising strategy to enhance the anti-tumor efficacy of ICB is to increase antigen exposure, thereby enhancing T cell activation and converting “cold” tumors into “hot” ones. Herein, we present an innovative all-in-one therapeutic nanoplatform to realize local mild photothermal- and photodynamic-triggered antigen exposure, thereby improving the anti-tumor efficacy of ICB. This nanoplatform involves conjugating programmed death-ligand 1 antibody (aPD-L1) with gadolinium-doped near-infrared (NIR)-emitting carbon dots (aPD-L1@GdCDs), which displays negligible cytotoxicity in the absence of light. But under controlled NIR laser irradiation, the GdCDs produce combined photothermal and photodynamic effects. This not only results in tumor ablation but also induces immunogenic cell death (ICD), facilitating enhanced infiltration of CD8+ T cells in the tumor area. Importantly, the combination of aPD-L1 with photothermal and photodynamic therapies via aPD-L1@GdCDs significantly boosts CD8+ T cell infiltration, reduces tumor size, and improves anti-metastasis effects compared to either GdCDs-based phototherapy or aPD-L1 alone. In addition, the whole treatment process can be monitored by multi-modal fluorescence/photoacoustic/magnetic resonance imaging (FLI/PAI/MRI). Our study highlights a promising nanoplatform for cancer diagnosis and therapy, as well as paves the way to promote the efficacy of ICB therapy through mild photothermal- and photodynamic-triggered immunotherapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of aPD-L1 conjugated with GdCDs. (B) TEM image of aPD-L1@GdCDs. Insert shows particle size distribution). (C) Zeta potential of GdCDs, aPD-L1, and aPD-L1@GdCDs. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (D) UV-Vis absorption of GdCDs, aPD-L1, and aPD-L1@GdCDs. (E) FL excitation and emission spectra of aPD-L1@GdCDs. Insert shows photograph of GdCDs in EP tube. (F) T1-weighted MR images with different Gd concentration and the corresponding relaxation rate (1/T1) as a function of Gd content. (G) PA values as a function of aPD-L1@GdCDs concentration. Insert shows the corresponding in vitro PA images. (H) IR images of aPD-L1@GdCDs under 650 nm laser at different power densities. (I) ESR spectra of GdCDs and aPD-L1@GdCDs under 650 nm laser irradiation.

(A) CLSM images of B16F10 cells treated with aPD-L1, GdCDs, and aPD-L1@GdCDs for 3 h. (B) CLSM images of B16F10 cells after incubation with blank, laser, aPD-L1@GdCDs, GdCDs + L, aPD-L1@GdCDs + L in the presence of DCFH-DA (650 nm laser, 0.15 W cm−2, 10 min). (C, D) Live/dead and apoptosis analysis of B16F10 cells after different treatments. (E) Relative expression of PD-L1 in B16F10 cells by measuring the intensity of FITC-labeled aPD-L1 after heating the cells in water bath at different temperatures. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (F) Western blot analysis of PD-L1 expression in B16F10 with different treatments. (G) Quantitative analysis of PD-L1 expression. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (H) Photographs of CD8+ T cells co-cultured with blank, GdCDs, aPD-L1, or aPD-L1@GdCDs pre-treated 4T1 cells. (I) Analysis of specific cytotoxicity of CD8+ T cells against pretreated cancer cells using a Calcein-AM assay. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01 and ****P < 0.0001, NS: nonsignificant difference.

(A) Schematic illustration of the transwell system experiment. (B) Analysis of intracellular ATP level after the above indicated treatment. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (C, D) Immunofluorescence analysis of HSP70 (green) and HMGB1 (green) expression after the above indicated treatment. (E) Flow cytometry plots of DCs (CD80+ CD86+) gated on CD11c+ DCs. (F) Secretion of TNF-α from the supernatant of DCs. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (G) The proliferation of CFSE-labeled T cells after incubation with treated DCs. (H) Secretion of INF-γ from the cell culture of T cells. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. *P < 0.05 and **P < 0.01. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) In vivo triple-modal imaging after intravenous injection with aPD-L1@GdCDs at different time intervals. (B-D) The corresponding signal intensity in tumor sites related to images. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (E) CLSM images of tumor tissues stained by FITC-conjugated secondary antibody (for aPD-L1) and Hoechst (for nuclei). (F, G) IR images and temperature elevation of tumor sites in B16F10 tumor model under laser irradiation (650 nm, 0.5 W cm−2). Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment.

(A) Schematic illustration of in vivo therapy for B16F10 tumor bearing mice. (B) Tumor volume of B16F10 mice being treated in different groups for 15 days. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. *P < 0.05, ***P < 0.001 and ****P < 0.0001. (C) Photographs of B16F10 mice and tumors extracted from mice at 15 days. (D, E) Survival rate and body weight of mice after various treatments. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (F) TUNEL and H&E staining of the B16F10 tumor tissues at day 7 post-treatment.

(A) Immunofluorescence analysis of PD-L1 (green), HSP70 (red), and HMGB1 (green) expression in B16F10 tumor tissues at day 5 post-treatment. (B) Representative flow cytometry plots of mature DCs in lymph nodes (CD80+ CD86+ gated on CD11c+ cells) on day 7 after different treatments. (C) Representative flow cytometry plots of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells (gated on CD3+ T cells) in the tumor at day 7 after different treatments. (D, E) Quantitative analysis of mature DCs, CD4+ and CD8+ T cells. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (F) Immunofluorescence images of CD8+ T cells in tumor tissue slices. (G, H) Contents of the IFN-γ and TNF-α in serum after the indicated therapy. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001 and ****P < 0.0001, NS: nonsignificant difference. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Schematic illustration of in vivo anti-tumor re-challenge in B16F10 tumor bearing mice. (B) Quantitative analysis of the Tem (CD3+ CD8+ CD44+ CD62L−) in the spleens of mice receiving various treatments. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01 and ****P < 0.0001. (C, D) Photographs and quantitative analysis of liver metastasis nodules. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment. (E) Survival rate of the B16F10 metastatic tumor model after different treatments. Data shown as Mean ± SD, n = 3 per treatment.
Schematic illustration of the synergistic phototherapy and ICB therapy via aPD-L1@GdCDs.

head.ce:title: Mild near-infrared laser-triggered photo-immunotherapy potentiates immune checkpoint blockade via an all-in-one theranostic nanoplatform

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: One of the primary challenges for immune checkpoint blockade (ICB)-based therapy is the limited infiltration of T lymphocytes (T cells) into tumors, often referred to as immunologically “cold” tumors. A promising strategy to enhance the anti-tumor efficacy of ICB is to increase antigen exposure, thereby enhancing T cell activation and converting “cold” tumors into “hot” ones. Herein, we present an innovative all-in-one therapeutic nanoplatform to realize local mild photothermal- and photodynamic-triggered antigen exposure, thereby improving the anti-tumor efficacy of ICB. This nanoplatform involves conjugating programmed death-ligand 1 antibody (aPD-L1) with gadolinium-doped near-infrared (NIR)-emitting carbon dots (aPD-L1@GdCDs), which displays negligible cytotoxicity in the absence of light. But under controlled NIR laser irradiation, the GdCDs produce combined photothermal and photodynamic effects. This not only results in tumor ablation but also induces immunogenic cell death (ICD), facilitating enhanced infiltration of CD8+ T cells in the tumor area. Importantly, the combination of aPD-L1 with photothermal and photodynamic therapies via aPD-L1@GdCDs significantly boosts CD8+ T cell infiltration, reduces tumor size, and improves anti-metastasis effects compared to either GdCDs-based phototherapy or aPD-L1 alone. In addition, the whole treatment process can be monitored by multi-modal fluorescence/photoacoustic/magnetic resonance imaging (FLI/PAI/MRI). Our study highlights a promising nanoplatform for cancer diagnosis and therapy, as well as paves the way to promote the efficacy of ICB therapy through mild photothermal- and photodynamic-triggered immunotherapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past decade, immunotherapy has emerged as a promising strategy in cancer treatment, leveraging the immune system to fight cancer. It offers distinct advantages, including reduced side effects and the potential for long-term immune memory [1–3]. Among various approaches, immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) has become a leading modality. ICB functions by blocking the interaction between programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) on cancer cells and programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1) on T lymphocytes (T cells). This blockade reverses immunosuppressive signals, thereby enhancing the cytotoxic anti-tumor activity of T cells [4–7]. However, recent studies have shown that the therapy efficacy of ICB is limited by the low T cell response in immunologically “cold” tumors [8,9]. Consequently, strategies are needed to elevate the T cell response in the tumor microenvironment (TME) and convert immunologically “cold” tumor to “hot” tumor [10,11].

Phototherapy, encompassing photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT), offers the advantages of non-invasiveness, high efficacy, spatiotemporal selectivity, and temporal control. Therefore, these techniques have been extensively investigated in the field of cancer therapeutics [12–15]. Nevertheless, high-temperature photothermal heating may lead to the destruction of surrounding normal tissues due to uncontrolled heat diffusion [16–18]. Mild PTT, which raises temperatures to around 45 °C, has been used in recent years for cancer treatment, yet its therapeutic effect alone is often limited [19,20]. On the other hand, oxygen-dependent PDT is constrained by the hypoxic condition within the TME [21–23]. Fortunately, synergistic PTT and PDT, when combined under high-penetration near-infrared (NIR) light, can offset each other's limitations and enhance overall therapeutic efficacy [24,25]. In addition, recent studies have provided evidence that both PTT and PDT are able to activate adaptive and innate anti-tumor immunity, as well as induce immunogenic cell death (ICD) [26–31]. The damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) released from damaged cells serve as a ‘danger’ signal that can enhance the phagocytosis of tumor-associated antigens (TAAs) by dendritic cells (DCs), and induce DC maturation and T cells activation, thus initiating anti-tumor immune responses and promoting infiltration of cytotoxic T cells [32–35]. Thus, combining mild photothermal/photodynamic treatments with anti-PD therapy is anticipated to overcome the above limitations. This approach can directly ablate tumors, shift the TME from “cold” to “hot” and enhance PD-1/PD-L1 blockade, thereby targeting residual tumor cells and improving overall efficacy [36,37]. However, most current approaches that combine anti-PD therapy with single PTT- or PDT-induced immunotherapy have demonstrated limited synergistic anti-tumor efficacy. Additionally, integrating these therapies with triple therapeutic functions often involves complex nanosystems [30,35,38–41].

Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered widespread attention as an excellent all-in-one multifunctional nanoplatform for cancer theranostics over the past years, owing to their good biocompatibility, high photostability, low toxicity, and ease of modification [42,43]. Under NIR laser irradiation, CDs have shown strong capabilities for PTT/PDT and photoacoustic imaging (PAI)/fluorescence imaging (FLI) [44]. Moreover, doping CDs with gadolinium ions (Gd3+) makes them excellent contrast agents for magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [45]. For example, Chen et al. developed Gd3+-functionalized CDs using a one-step hydrothermal method with citric acid as the carbon source, ethylenediamine as a passivation agent, and Gd3+ as a linking agent. These nanodots demonstrated stable fluorescence and a longitudinal relaxivity (r1) of 6.06 mM−1 s−1 as an MRI contrast agent [46]. Similarly, Yu et al. employed diethylenetriamine instead of ethylenediamine to prepare Gd3+-doped CDs, which exhibited high fluorescence and improved r1 value of 14.33 mM−1 s−1 for T1-weighted MRI [47]. However, those probes emit blue and green light, which have limited tissue penetration, making them less suitable for advanced biomedical applications.

In this work, we developed an all-in-one therapeutic nanosystem that integrates Gd3+-doped red-emissive CDs conjugated with aPD-L1 antibody (aPD-L1@GdCDs) for multi-modal cancer imaging, PTT/PDT and phototherapy-sensitized ICB (Scheme 1 ). After intravenous injection, the aPD-L1@GdCDs effectively targeted tumor cells overexpressing PD-L1, resulting in enhanced internalization of the nanoplatform at the tumor site. Under mild NIR laser irradiation, the aPD-L1@GdCDs simultaneously induced PTT and PDT effects. These not only directly killed tumor cells but also stimulated a robust immune response through cytokine secretion and antigen presentation, leading to DC maturation-mediated T cell activation and cytotoxic T cell infiltration. Additionally, the local mild temperature increase facilitated the controlled release of aPD-L1 for ICB. The aPD-L1 antibody effectively downregulated PD-L1 expression on tumor cells, thereby disrupting the PD-1/PD-L1 interaction to reverse immunosuppression and further boost T cell activation and infiltration. Guided by multi-modal FLI/PAI/MRI, the in vivo anti-tumor study demonstrated the high efficacy of this nanoplatform in enhancing cytotoxic T cell infiltration, tumor ablation, and anti-metastasis. This approach offers a promising strategy for augmenting ICB therapy in immunologically “cold” tumors.

All these chemicals used were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification. Glutathione (GSH) and Annexin V-FITC/PI apoptosis detection kit were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Formamide and Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). PD-L1 antibody (aPD-L1) was acquired from Bio X cell (Lebanon, NH, USA). FITC anti-mouse PD-L1 antibody (FITC-aPD-L1) and (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3-carboxymethoxyphenyl)-2-(4-sulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium) (MTS) cell proliferation assay kits were obtained from Novus Biologicals (Centennial, CO, USA). 2′,7′-Dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), live/deadTM cell imaging kit, dynabeads mouse T-activator CD3/CD28, Hoechst 33342, and all cell culture reagents were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA). JC-1 assay kit was acquired from Beyotime (Shanghai, China). Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) assay kit was purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). MojoSortTM mouse naïve T cell isolation kit, PE Rat anti-mouse FOXP3, and APC Rat anti-mouse CD25 were purchased from Biolegend (San Diego, CA, USA). The PE Rat anti-mouse CD8a, FITC Rat anti-mouse CD4, Percp-Cy5.5 anti-mouse CD3e, FITC anti-mouse CD86, and PE anti-mouse CD206, APC anti-mouse PD-L1 were procured from BD Biosciences (San Jose, California, USA). IFN-γ ELISA kit and TNF-α ELISA kit were purchased from Abcam (Cambridge, UK). Mouse PD-L1/B7-H1 ELISA kit was purchased from Boster Bio (CA, USA). Deionized water (DI H2O) with a resistivity of 18.2 ΜΩ was purified by Millipore Milli-Q and used throughout this work.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of CDs were characterized using an Ultrospec 2100 pro spectrophotometer (Amersham Biosciences, UK). The Zeta potential and hydrodynamic size measurements were carried out with a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, USA), using the mode of Phase Analysis Light Scattering at room temperature. An Edinburgh FLS920P Analytical Instrument (Edinburgh, UK) with a xenon lamp in 450 W steady-state, an Nd-pumped OPOlette excited laser source and calibrated integrating sphere was used to measure the photoluminescence (PL). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL-2100F, JEOL Ltd., Japan) with Oxford Instrument energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) system at 200 kV operating voltage was performed to obtain the morphology of nanoparticles. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images and Raman spectra were carried out with Bruker Nanoscope 8 (Bruker, UK) and Raman Spectroscopy (Renishaw, UK), respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Nexsa G2 surface analysis system, Thermo fisher, USA) was performed on an Axis Ultra DLD spectrograph with Al/Kα as the source. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were carried out with Bruker Vertex-70 FTIR. Ultrasonic Cleaner (DAIHAN Scientific, Korea) was used to promote the dispersion of solution. In vitro FLI was captured by a Leica TCS SPE Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (CLSM, Leica Microsystems, Germany). The flow cytometry was analyzed by BD Accuri C6 Flow Cytometer (BD Biosciences, USA). PAI was measured by Vevo LAZR Multi-modality Imaging Platform (Fujifilm Visualsonics, Canada) with an integrated fiberoptic linear array LZ250 transducer. T1-weighted relaxivity and MRI were measured by 3T MRI Instrument (Bruker, Germany). In vivo FLI was carried out by an IVIS Lumina Series III Pre-clinical In Vivo Animal Imaging System (PerkinElmer, USA).

The Gd3+-doped CDs were synthesized via a modified one-step hydrothermal method, using GSH as the carbon source. In brief, 0.28 g of GSH was first added into formamide (3 % wt) and mixed with varying concentrations of Gd3+ (0 % or 1 % wt of carbon source). The solution was first sonicated to form a transparent mixture, which was then transferred to a two-neck round-bottom flask and placed in a microwave chemical reactor (Kerui Instruments, China) equipped with reflow devices. The reactor was set to 160 °C for 2 min with stirring. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the reaction products were collected and diluted 2-fold. The solution was then dialyzed against DI H2O using a membrane with a 3.5 kDa cut-off molecular weight for 4–5 days to remove any residuals and smaller molecules. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μM Millipore membrane to remove the larger particles. Subsequently, the CDs and GdCDs were obtained by drying overnight in a vacuum oven.

aPD-L1@GdCDs were obtained by electrostatic adsorption. Briefly, aPD-L1 was mixed with GdCDs at a mass ratio of 1:10 and stirred overnight. Excess GdCDs were removed by ultracentrifugation through Amicon® Ultra Centrifugal Filter (Merk, Germany). The loading efficacy of aPD-L1 was quantified through a mouse PD-L1/B7-H1 ELISA Kit according to the manufacturer protocol. Finally, the aPD-L1@GdCDs were stored at 4 °C.

To investigate the photothermal performance, PE tubes containing GdCDs or aPD-L1@GdCDs were irradiated with 650 nm laser at different power densities at room temperature. The real-time temperature was recorded every 20 s, and thermal images were captured with an infrared (IR) thermal camera. To evaluate the photostability, GdCDs were irradiated at a power density of 0.75 W cm−2 for five cycles of 5 min each, followed by cooling to room temperature. During the process, real-time temperature and images were also recorded.

The 4T1 mouse mammary carcinoma cell line, B16F10 mouse melanoma stable cell line, green fluorescent protein (GFP)-labeled B16F10 mouse melanoma cell line (GFP-labeled B16F10), human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC), macrophage cell line RAW 264.7 and JAWSII murine immature dendritic cell line were all purchased from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA). The 4T1, B16F10, and GFP-labeled B16F10 cells cultured in Roswell Park Memorial Institute 1640 medium (RPMI 1640) supplemented with 10 % (v/v) fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % Penicillin/Streptomycin (P/S). HUVEC were cultured in Human Large Vessel Endothelial Cell Basal Medium supplemented with 10 % (v/v) FBS and 1 % P/S. RAW264.7 cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % (v/v) FBS and 1 % P/S. JAWSII cells were cultured in Minimum Essential Medium-alpha (MEM-α, nucleosides) supplemented with 20 % (v/v) FBS, 2 mM l-gluta mine, 5 ng mL−1 recombinant Murine Granulocyte-Macrophage Colony-Stimulating Factor (GM-CSF), and 1 % P/S to maintain their viability. All cells were incubated at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5 % CO2.

A standard MTS assay was conducted to evaluate the cytotoxicities of GdCDs and aPD-L1@GdCDs. Generally, 4T1 or B16F10 cells (1 × 104 per well) were seeded in 96-well cell culture plates and allowed to adhere overnight. We then treated the cells with varying concentrations (0–200 μg mL−1) of GdCDs or aPD-L1@GdCDs, with each concentration tested in triplicate wells. Notably, GdCDs and aPD-L1@GdCDs had the same content of GdCDs. After incubating for 24 h, the cytotoxicity was determined at 492 nm using Varioskan LUX multimode microplate reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), following the standard protocol of the MTS assay.

4T1 or B16F10 cells (1 × 105 per well) were seeded in 35 mm glass-bottom dishes and incubated with blank, GdCDs, FITC-aPD-L1, and FITC-aPD-L1@GdCDs for 3 h. Here, FITC-aPD-L1 was used to enable visualization of aPD-L1 by CLSM. FITC-aPD-L1@GdCDs were prepared using the same method as that of aPD-L1@GdCDs, ensuring a GdCDs concentration of 100 μg mL−1 in each group. After rinsing twice with PBS, the cell nucleus was stained with Hoechst for 5 min. The CLSM images and flow cytometry analysis were taken to visualize the internalization. In addition, flow cytometry analysis was also conducted to evaluate the time-dependent endocytosis of FITC-aPD-L1@GdCDs at various time points (0, 1, 2, 3, and 5 h).

4T1 or B16F10 cells (1 × 105 per well) were seeded in 35 mm glass-bottom dishes and treated with blank, laser, aPD-L1@GdCDs, GdCDs + L, and aPD-L1@GdCDs + L. After 3 h of sample internalization, the cells were subjected to 650 nm laser with a density of 0.15 W cm−2 for 10 min. Subsequently, the cells were stained with the ROS indicator (DCFH-DA, 10 μM) for 30 min and images were captured by CLSM under the excitation wavelength of 488 nm. The fluorescence data were collected ranging from 500 to 550 nm.

The expression of PD-L1 in cancer cells (B16F10 and 4T1) and normal cells (HUVEC) was firstly evaluated. Briefly, three kinds of cells (1 × 104 per well) with three parallels were seeded in 96-well plate and allowed adhered overnight. The cells were treated with FITC-labeled aPD-L1 at room temperature for 1 h. The fluorescence of each well was measured at an excitation wavelength of 488 nm using a microplate reader. The targeting efficiency of aPD-L1 and the blocking effect on PD-L1 in B16F10 cells were quantitatively assessed using flow cytometry. B16F10 cells (1 × 105 per well) were seeded in 12-well plate. One set of cells were treated with blank, FITC- aPD-L1, GdCDs, and FITC-aPD-L1@GdCDs for 3 h. Another set of B16F10 cells was treated with blank, aPD-L1, GdCDs, and aPD-L1@GdCDs for 3 h, which were further incubated with FITC-labeled aPD-L1 for 1 h at room temperature. The fluorescence intensity of FITC revealed the targeted aPD-L1 and PD-L1 residuals. Then, the effects of different treatment groups on PD-L1 expression in B16F10 cells were quantified by western blot. Briefly, B16F10 cells (1 × 106 per well) were seeded in 6-well plates and treated with blank, laser, aPD-L1@GdCDs, GdCDs + L, aPD-L1@GdCDs + L. After 3 h of sample internalization, a 650 nm laser was administered at a power density of 0.5 W cm−2 for 10 min. Cells were lysed in RIPA buffer containing 1 mM PMSF on ice for 30 min. The lysates were centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C, and the supernatant containing the collected protein was quantified with BCA assay (Thermo Fisher scientific, USA). Protein samples were mixed with loading buffer and boiled at 95 °C for 10 min, separated by 10 % SDS-PAGE gel electrophoresis, and transferred onto nitrocellulose (NC) membrane (Invitrogen, USA). After blocking in QuickBlockTM buffer (Beyotime, China), the membrane was incubated with primary PD-L1 antibody and antibody against β-actin overnight at 4 °C, followed by HRP-labeled secondary antibody for 1 h at room temperature. The blots were visualized by using a ChemiDoc MP gel imaging system (Bio-Rad, USA).

To investigate the therapeutic efficacy of aPD-L1@GdCDs, 4T1 cells (1 × 105 per well) were seeded in 35 mm glass-bottom dishes and treated with blank, laser (0.75 W cm−2), GdCDs, GdCDs + L0.15 (PDT), and GdCDs + L0.75 (PDT/PTT). The laser power density in PDT and PDT/PTT groups were 0.15 W cm−2 and 0.75 W cm−2, respectively. B16F10 cells (1 × 105 per well) were treated with blank, laser, aPD-L1@GdCDs, GdCDs + L, and aPD-L1@GdCDs + L. After 10 min of 650 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W cm−2), the cells were stained with live/dead kit according to manufacturer’s report and captured by CLSM.

4T1 or B16F10 cells (1 × 105 per well) were seeded in 24-well cell culture plates and treated with the aforementioned groups. Light irradiation was performed upon 650 nm laser for 10 min. Afterwards, the cells were collected to stain with propidium iodide (PI) and Annexin V-FITC. Flow cytometry was performed to analyze the apoptosis & necrosis according to the manufacturer’s instructions.

B16F10 cells (1 × 105 per well) were seeded into the 35 mm glass-bottom dishes and treated with blank, laser, aPD-L1@GdCDs, GdCDs + L, and aPD-L1@GdCDs + L. The 650 nm laser at power density of 0.5 W cm−2 was performed for 10 min. The cells were then stained with JC-1 (5 μM) at 37 °C for 20 min and observed by CLSM according to manufacturer’s report.

To determine the therapy induced ICD, the release of DAMPs such as heat shock protein 70 (HSP70), high-mobility group box 1 (HMGB1), and ATP was examined. Briefly, B16F10 cells (1 × 105 per well) were seeded in a 35 mm glass-bottom dishes and allowed different treatments (blank, laser, aPD-L1@GdCDs, GdCDs + L, and aPD-L1@GdCDs + L). A 650 nm laser at power density of 0.5 W cm−2 was performed for 10 min. The supernatant was collected at 4 h post-treatment to measure the ATP level using a commercially available ATP assay kit following the manufacturer's instructions. Meanwhile, the adherent cells were washed with PBS, fixed with 4 % Paraformaldehyde (PFA) solution (ExCell Bio. Ltd.) for 20 min, permeabilized with 0.1 % Triton X for 5 min, and then incubated with HSP70 and HMGB1 anti-rabbit monoclonal antibodies for 1 h, followed by staining with Alexa Fluor 488 goat anti-rabbit IgG for 1 h. After staining the nuclei with Hoechst for 5 min, the cells were captured by CLSM.

To investigate the degree of DCs maturation, JAWS II cell line was used. Typically, B16F10 cells (2 × 104 per well) were seeded in the upper chamber of a 24-well tanswell plate and allowed adhered overnight. The cells were then pretreated under different conditions (blank, laser, aPD-L1@GdCDs, GdCDs + L, and aPD-L1@GdCDs + L) with or without 650 nm laser irradiation (0.5 W cm−2) for 10 min, followed by co-culture with immature JAWS II (1 × 105 per well) in the bottom well for an additional 24 h. The maturation degree of JAWS II was measured by flow cytometry after staining with Perp 5.5 anti-mouse CD11c, FITC anti-mouse CD80, and PE- anti-mouse CD86 antibodies. Additionally, the cytokine of TNF-α secreted by JAWS II in the culture supernatant was quantified using a TNF-α ELISA kit according to the manufacturer’s protocol.

M2 macrophages were induced from RAW264.7 cells (M0) by adding IL-4 (20 ng mL−1, Invitrogen, Carlsbad, CA) for 24 h. To assess the expression of M2-associated genes (IL-10, Arg-1, CD206, and Fizz-1), RNA from macrophages (M0 and M2) was extracted using RNAiso Plus (TaKaRa), quantified with NanoDrop (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), and reverse-transcribed using PrimeScript RT reagent Kit (Perfect Real Time, TaKaRa). Quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR) was conducted using TB Green Premix Ex Taq (Tli RNaseH Plus, TaKaRa) on a CFX96 Touch Real-Time PCR Detection System (Bio-Rad, USA) with specific primers [48]. To explore the repolarization of M2 to M1 macrophages, M2 macrophages (1 × 105 per well) were placed in the lower chamber of a transwell setup. B16F10 cells (2 × 104 per well), pre-treated with various conditions (blank, laser, aPD-L1@GdCDs, GdCDs + L, aPD-L1@GdCDs + L), were seeded above and subjected to 650 nm laser irradiation at 0.5 W cm−2 for 10 min as needed. After 48 h, the macrophages were stained and analyzed for M1 (CD86) and M2 (CD206) markers using flow cytometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The activity of CD8+ T cells in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Naïve CD8+ T cells were firstly isolated from the spleen of Balb/c mice using MojoSortTM mouse naïve T cell isolation kit and then activated by CD3/CD28 T activator DynabeadsTM according to the manufacturer’s instruction. Then, B16F10 cells (1 × 105 per well) were seeded in 6-well plate and allowed to incubate with different samples (blank, GdCDs, aPD-L1, and aPD-L1@GdCDs) for 3 h. The activated T cells were stained with CellTraceTM CSFE cell proliferation kit (Invitrogen, USA) and then co-cultured with cancer cells at a ratio of 1:1 for 24 h. After gently washing with PBS, the captured images were obtained using fluorescence microscope (Nikon, USA). Meanwhile, the proliferation of T cells in the cell culture supernatant was quantified using flow cytometry.

To investigate the specific toxicity of CD8+ T cells towards cancer cells, a calcein-AM rendition assay was conducted according to previously published protocol. Briefly, cancer cells (1 × 104 per well) were seeded into a U-bottom 96-well plate and labeled with calcein-AM in serum and phenol red-free medium at 37 °C for 30 min. After incubation with different samples for 3 h, the cancer cells were treated with or without 650 nm laser irradiation at power density of 0.5 W cm−2 for 10 min. Then the activated CD8+ T cells (1 × 105 per well) were added. Positive (retention in medium) and negative (retention maximal lysis) controls were treated with phenol red-free medium and lysis buffer (1 % Triton X-100), respectively. After another 24 h incubation, the plate was washed twice with PBS and the fluorescence value at 488 nm was read out by a microplate reader. The cytotoxicity of CD8+ T cells was calculated according to the previous method. In addition, CSFE labeled T cells were incubated with above stimulated DCs for 48 h, and the proliferation of T cells were measured by flow cytometry. The secreted INF-γ from T cells in the supernatant was quantified by an INF-γ ELISA kit according to the manufacturer’s instrument.

All animal protocols were conducted under the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of Department of Health of the Government of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region. An animal ethics approval was obtained from the Animal Ethics Committee of The Hong Kong Polytechnic University (Ref No.: 21-22/23-BME-R-OTHERS) with an animal license issued by the Department of Health of the Government of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region (Ref No.: (23-96) in DH/HT&A/8/2/4Pt.13). Balb/c mice and nude mice aged 4–5 weeks were subcutaneously inoculated with B16F10 (or GFP-labeled B16F10) and 4 T1 cells (1 × 106), respectively, to establish tumor models in the upper thigh. The tumor volume was calculated as length × width2 /2 and allowed to reach a size of approximately 80–120 mm3 by day 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo imaging
ce:section.ce:para: 0.1 mL GdCDs and aPD-L1@GdCDs in equal content of GdCDs (2 mg mL−1) were intravenously injected into B16F10 tumor model (Balb/c mice) and 4 T1 tumor model (Balb/c nude mice), respectively. The in vivo fluorescence images were acquired at different time points (0–48h) using an IVIS system (excitation at 620 nm and emission at 670 nm).

For in vitro PAI study, different concentrations (0–2 mg mL−1) of aPD-L1@GdCDs were injected into capillary tubes submerged in DI H2O. PA images were acquired using an integrated fiberoptic linear array LZ250 transducer with an axial resolution of 75 μm at a frequency of 21 MHz. For in vivo PAI, 0.1 mL aPD-L1@GdCDs were intravenously injected into B16F10 tumor model (Balb/c mice), and PA signals were collected at time points of 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 24, and 48 h, respectively.

T1-weighted relaxation time of GdCDs was measured in vitro using PE tubes with different concentrations of GdCDs. The relaxivity value was obtained from the slope of the linear fitting of 1/T relaxation time (s−1) versus Gd concentration (mM). T1-weighted images were acquired with the following parameters: TE = 10 ms, TR = 450 ms, field of view (FOV) = 217 mm, flip angle (FA) = 90°, and the slice thickness = 3 mm. The intravital T1-weight MR images were captured at 0, 2, 4, 6, and 8 h time points after intravenous injection of 0.1 mL aPD-L1@GdCDs into B16F10 tumor model (Balb/c mice).

In the B16F10 tumor model, seven groups (3 mice per group) received various treatments: blank, laser, GdCDs, aPD-L1, aPD-L1@GdCDs, GdCDs + L, and aPD-L1@GdCDs + L, respectively, at an equivalent dose of 0.2 mg GdCDs and 25 μg aPD-L1 via intravenous injection. At 2 h (for group of GdCDs + L) or 4 h (for group of aPD-L1@GdCDs + L) post-treatment, the tumor sites were irradiated with 650 nm laser at power density of 0.5 W cm−2 for 10 min. The treatment was repeated once on day 3. The photo-thermal images were captured by IR thermal camera during treatment. The tumor size and body weight of each mouse were measured at 3-day intervals for 15 days. At the end of experiment, tumors were excised and photographed.

For the 4 T1 tumor model, 5 groups of mice were treated with: (i) blank, (ii) GdCDs, (iii) laser (0.75 W cm−2), (iv) GdCDs + L0.15 (PDT), (v) GdCDs + L0.75 (PDT/PTT). At 2 h post-injection of 0.1 mL GdCDs (2 mg mL−1) via tail vein, the tumor sites were exposed to 650 nm laser for 10 min. The photothermal images were captured by IR thermal camera during treatment. The tumor size and body weight were measured every 2 days until day 14 when tumors were excised and photographed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ex vivo analysis of immune response
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the DC maturation and T cell infiltration in vivo, mice were sacrificed 7 days post-treatment, and their tumor, spleen, and lymph nodes were harvested to prepare single cell suspension for flow cytometry analysis. Briefly, the tumor and draining lymph nodes were fragmented and incubated with 1 mg mL−1 collagenase D (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) in pure RPMI 1640 at 37 °C for 1 h, respectively, and the cell suspension was obtained by removing the undigested tissues through 70 μm cell strainer. For the spleen, the organ was minced using a syringe plunger and passed through 70 μm cell strainer. The red blood cells in the suspension were lysed by 1X RBC Lysis Buffer (Invitrogen, USA). After cell counting, the cells collected from all these organs were specific blocked with anti-CD16/CD32 antibody and stained with different antibodies for identification of different population. For the analysis of T cells in tumor, the cells were stained with Perp anti-mouse CD3e, FITC anti-mouse CD4, and PE anti-mouse CD8a. To analyze maturation of DCs in lymph node, the cells were stained with APC anti-mouse CD3e, FITC anti-mouse CD80, and PE anti-mouse CD86. To investigate the immune memory T cells, the cells from spleen were stained with Perp anti-mouse CD3e, PE anti-mouse CD44, and FITC anti-mouse CD62 antibody. The populations of effector memory T cells (Tem) were marked as CD3+ CD44+ CD62L−.

For immunohistochemical staining, the tumors were collected after treatment for 7 days, which were then preserved in 4 % PFA and embedded in paraffin. Sections of 7 μm thickness were prepared and blocked with QuickBlockTM buffer for 30 min. To determine the expression of PD-L1, the sections were stained overnight at 4 °C with FITC-PD-L1 antibody. To detect therapy induced ICD, the sections were stained overnight at 4 °C with primary antibodies against HSP70 and HMGB1, followed by incubation with FITC-labeled secondary antibody for 1 h. To localize the cytotoxic CD8+ T cells, the sections were stained overnight at 4 °C with anti-mouse CD8a antibody, followed by incubation with FITC-labeled secondary antibody for 1 h. All sections were observed under CLSM after nucleus staining by Hoechst for another 10 min. To specifically analyze PD-L1 blockade, GFP-labeled B16F10 tumor model was established. After various treatments, GFP-labeled B16F10 tumors were processed for cell isolation by GFP-based sorting via FACSAria III Cell Sorter (BD Biosciences, USA). Isolated cancer cells were stained with APC anti-mouse PD-L1 for flow cytometry analysis and further validated using a PD-L1 ELISA.

At day 7 post-treatment, the blood samples were collected from the mice in each group. The serum was separated by centrifugation and analyzed for TNF-α and IFN-γ using ELISA kits according to the manufacture’s protocol.

The main organs were harvested at two weeks post-treatment, and tumors were collected at 7 days post-treatment. All tissues were preserved in 4 % PFA solution, embedded in paraffin blocks, sliced, and stained with H&E according to manufacturer’s protocol. The apoptotic cells in the tumor sections were determined using a TUNEL kit according to manufacturer’s protocol.

At day 7 post-treatment, mice were intravenously injected with B16F10 (2 × 105) via the tail vein. Livers were harvested at day 21, and the number of metastatic nodules was recorded. Parallel experiments were also conducted to investigate the survival rate of the mice.

All experiments were carried out at least three times and the data were given as Mean ± SD. Significant differences were calculated using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) in GraphPad Prism 8.0 software. Statistical significance was set at *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001. NS: nonsignificant difference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, synthesis and characterization of aPD-L1@GdCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Gd3+-doped red-emissive CDs were initially synthesized using GSH as the carbon source through an improved microwave-assisted heating method [49]. The synthesized GdCDs (1 % Gd) displayed favorable water dispersibility, with an average size of approximately 3.2 ± 0.4 nm as determined by analyzing the TEM and AFM images (Fig. S1). The XPS spectrum of GdCDs prominently displayed a characteristic peak of Gd4d at approximately 142 eV, which was absent in the XPS spectrum of the unmodified CDs (Figs. S2 and S3). This distinct peak confirmed the successful chelation of Gd3+ onto the CDs (Figs. S4 and S5). Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) quantified Gd content (wt%) at 0 % in CDs and 3.77 % in GdCDs. The typical peaks of Gd alongside carbon and other relevant elements were clearly observed in the EDS spectrum, further verifying the successful doping of Gd3+ into the CDs (Fig. S6). The Raman spectra exhibited peaks at 1590 cm−1 and 1373 cm−1, indicating the presence of both sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons (Fig. S7).

The fabrication of aPD-L1@GdCDs involved the conjugation of aPD-L1 onto GdCDs via electrostatic adsorption (Fig. 1 A). The resulting aPD-L1@GdCDs exhibited an increased size of approximately 7.9 ± 1.6 nm and a change in zeta potential from −14 mV to −2 mV, indicating the successful functionalization of aPD-L1 (Fig. 1B, C). The UV–vis absorption spectra of aPD-L1@GdCDs further confirmed this conjugation, revealing a characteristic protein-related peak at 260 nm (Fig. 1D). Complementing these findings, the FTIR spectrum of aPD-L1@GdCDs revealed new characteristic peaks at 930–1180 cm−1 and 2840–2970 cm−1 attributable to both aPD-L1 and GdCDs, confirming the successful binding of aPD-L1 to GdCDs (Fig. S8). Given the intrinsic photoacoustic and fluorescence properties of CDs and the paramagnetic characteristics of Gd3+, the synthesized aPD-L1@GdCDs demonstrated considerable potential for multi-modal imaging techniques, including PAI, FLI and MRI. As shown in Fig. 1E, the aPD-L1@GdCDs demonstrated excitation-dependent emission spectra, with the maximum emission intensity observed at 650 nm when excited at 420 nm. Moreover, the contrast and brightness of T1-weighted MR images of aPD-L1@GdCDs improved with increasing concentrations of Gd, ranging from 0 to 488 μM (Fig. 1F). The longitudinal relaxation rate showed a linear relationship with Gd concentration, with a calculated relaxivity (r1) of 25.3 mM−1 s−1. This value is notably higher than that of commercially available contrast agents, highlighting the significant potential of aPD-L1@GdCDs for enhanced MRI applications. Furthermore, the photoacoustic performance of aPD-L1@GdCDs was assessed by measuring its photoacoustic signals in capillary tubes. As shown in Fig. 1G, a concentration-dependent increase of photoacoustic signal was observed for aPD-L1@GdCDs, demonstrating the feasibility of aPD-L1@GdCDs as a contrast agent for PAI.

The photothermal properties of aPD-L1@GdCDs were evaluated by monitoring the temperature changes under 650 nm laser irradiation. The results, illustrated in Fig. 1H, showed that the temperature increase was dependent on both the irradiation time and the power density of the laser. Notably, aPD-L1@GdCDs at a concentration of 100 μg mL−1 rapidly reached 50 °C within 5 min when exposed to a mild laser power density of 0.5 W cm−2 (Fig. S9). Besides, the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of GdCDs was calculated to be 56 %, based on the temperature change kinetics. No significant change in photothermal performance was observed after 5 cycles of laser irradiation, indicating excellent reproducibility and stability of aPD-L1@GdCDs in photothermal conversion (Fig. S10). The type of ROS generated under 650 nm laser irradiation was assessed using the electron spin resonance (ESR) technique with 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidone (TMPD) as the spin-trapping agent [50]. Characteristic peaks with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1 were observed for both GdCDs and aPD-L1@GdCDs, indicating their ability to produce singlet oxygen (1O2) for PDT (Fig. 1I).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake, cytotoxicity and hemolysis analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Following systematic physicochemical characterization, the intracellular uptake of aPD-L1@GdCDs was initially examined in B16F10 and 4T1 cancer cells. Fluorescent analysis with FITC-labeled aPD-L1 showed that the expression of PD-L1 was significantly higher in these cancer cells (B16F10 and 4T1) compared to normal cells (HUVEC) (Fig. S11). The time-dependent cellular uptake of aPD-L1@GdCDs was evaluated at various time points (0, 1, 2, 3, and 5 h) using flow cytometry to measure the fluorescence signal of GdCDs. The results indicated that the cellular uptake of aPD-L1@GdCDs increased with the duration of incubation (Fig. S12). After 3 h of incubation, both green and red fluorescence signals were detected within the cells, indicating efficient cellular uptake of aPD-L1@GdCDs (Fig. 2 A and Fig. S13). Here, green fluorescence signals indicated the effective recognition of PD-L1 on the cancer cell membrane, while red fluorescence signals originated from GdCDs. The red fluorescence intensity of aPD-L1@GdCDs in the cytoplasm was significantly higher compared to that of GdCDs alone. This observation suggested that the binding of aPD-L1 to PD-L1 in cancer cells with high PD-L1 expression enhanced endocytosis, which may play a crucial role in preventing the unwanted recycling of PD-L1. These selective intracellular uptake results were further confirmed by flow cytometry analysis (Fig. S14). Subsequently, MTS and hemolysis assays were conducted. As shown in Fig. S15, both GdCDs and aPD-L1@GdCDs displayed negligible cytotoxicity even at high concentrations in the absence of laser irradiation. In addition, no significant hemolysis was observed in PBS containing various concentrations of aPD-L1@GdCDs, demonstrating the excellent biocompatibility of aPD-L1@GdCDs (Fig. S16).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro immunogenic tumor cell killing capacity mediated by aPD-L1@GdCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The intracellular production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) was evaluated using DCFH-DA probe, which is oxidized to form fluorescent 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein (DCF). Upon laser irradiation, B16F10 cells treated with GdCDs or aPD-L1@GdCDs exhibited intense green fluorescence, indicating a robust intracellular ROS generation capability (Fig. 2B). Utilizing the significant ROS production and photothermal properties, the in vitro phototherapeutic efficacy of the synthesized aPD-L1@GdCDs against B16F10 or 4T1 cancer cells was evaluated. The 4T1 cells were subjected to different treatments: blank, laser (0.75 W cm−2), GdCDs, GdCDs + L0.15 (PDT), and GdCDs + L0.75 (PDT and PTT). A low laser power density of 0.15 W cm−2 was specifically employed for the PDT group, while a higher laser power density of 0.75 W cm−2 was utilized for the PTT+PDT group. Notably, both GdCDs + L0.15 and GdCDs + L0.75 groups displayed similar levels of green fluorescence, suggesting that high laser power density was not required for effective ROS generation (Fig. S17). The live/dead assay and flow cytometry results for both B16F10 and 4T1 cancer cells revealed a significant decrease in cell viability in the high-power density group compared to the low-power density group, highlighting the synergistic effect of PDT and PTT in cancer cell eradication (Fig. 2C, 2D, S18, and S19). However, a small proportion of cancer cells survived the combined mild phototherapy, which raises concerns about the potential cancer recurrence. The loss of MMP is a well-known indicator of mitochondrial dysfunction, commonly linked to cell apoptosis. To detect changes in MMP in B16F10 cancer cells following treatment, the fluorescent MMP indicator JC-1 was employed. The results demonstrated that cancer cells treated with GdCDs and aPD-L1@GdCDs under laser irradiation exhibited showed significantly reduced MMP compared to the control groups, suggesting the presence of mitochondrial dysfunction (Fig. S20).

It is well-documented that elevated temperatures generated during phototherapy can upregulate the expression of PD-L1 on the surface of cancer cells, contributing to increased immunosuppression. Our results similarly demonstrated that mild heating in a water bath from 37 °C to 47 °C resulted in enhanced PD-L1 expression in B16F10 cells, which may facilitate stronger binding between PD-L1 and aPD-L1 for ICB (Fig. 2E). The application of aPD-L1@GdCDs may effectively promote ICB on tumor cells during phototherapy, thereby improving therapeutic efficacy through immunotherapy. To further investigate the efficiency of PD-L1 blockade under mild phototherapy using aPD-L1@GdCDs, B16F10 cells were treated with various controls: blank, laser only, and GdCDs as negative control groups, and aPD-L1 only and aPD-L1@GdCDs as positive control groups. Experimental groups included GdCDs + L and aPD-L1@GdCDs + L for comparison. Western blot analysis of PD-L1 expression demonstrated that the GdCDs + L group significantly increased PD-L1 levels compared to negative control groups, indicating that photo-initiated hyperthermia induced adaptive immune resistance (Fig. 2F, G). In contrast, the aPD-L1@GdCDs + L group showed a significant reduction in PD-L1 expression, effectively downregulating PD-L1 expression even in the presence of laser stimulation. These findings underscore the efficient immunoblocking capability of aPD-L1@GdCDs on PD-L1 upregulated tumor cells during phototherapy.

To investigate the ability of aPD-L1@GdCDs to enhance T cell recruitment and infiltration for tumor immunotherapy, cancer cells were pre-treated with blank, GdCDs, aPD-L1, and aPD-L1@GdCDs, and then co-cultured with pre-activated CD8+ T cells. After 24 h of co-culture, non-captured CD8+ T cells were removed by washing. Fluorescence images indicated that the number of T cells bound to cancer cells in the aPD-L1 and aPD-L1@GdCDs treated groups was significantly higher compared to the other groups (Fig. 2H). This finding suggested enhanced recognition of cancer cells by T cells following PD-L1 blockade. Additionally, the morphology of the cancer cells appeared atrophic, indicating increased susceptibility to T cell-mediated attack after PD-L1/PD-1 blockade. Building on these observations, we explored the enhanced therapeutic effects of combining immunotherapy with phototherapy using aPD-L1@GdCDs. A Calcein-AM assay was employed to assess the viability of cancer cells co-cultured with CD8+ T cells across various treatment groups [51]. The results indicated that the CD8+ T cell-only group slightly decreased cancer cell viability compared to the blank control group, demonstrating that CD8+ T cells alone were insufficient to efficiently kill cancer cells due to the PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint pathway (Fig. 2I). In contrast, the combination of aPD-L1@GdCDs with CD8+ T cells resulted in a significant reduction in cancer cell viability, highlighting the ICB effect. Notably, integrating aPD-L1@GdCDs with both mild phototherapy and immunotherapy (aPD-L1@GdCDs + L + T) further reduced cell viability from 33 % to 12 % compared to the mild phototherapy group (aPD-L1@GdCDs + L). These results demonstrate a synergistic effect when combining aPD-L1 with mild phototherapy, leading to highly effective targeting of cancer cells and suppression of immune checkpoint pathways.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phototherapy-triggered T cell activation in transwell model
ce:section.ce:para: The synergistic mechanism of photo-immune therapy utilizing aPD-L1@GdCDs is predicated on two key components: (1) ICB of tumor cells exhibiting upregulated PD-L1 expression during phototherapy, facilitated by the binding of aPD-L1 to PD-L1, which increases the vulnerability of tumor cells to T cell-mediated attack; and (2) Phototherapy-induced ICD activates T cells and enhances their infiltration into the tumor. The first component was demonstrated in the previous section. To further investigate the activation of T cells triggered by phototherapy via aPD-L1@GdCDs, we employed a two-step incubation transwell model (Fig. 3 A).

Immature DCs in the lower chamber were initially incubated with B16F10 cancer cells that had been pre-treated with laser irradiation in the upper chamber. Phototherapy releases various DAMPs, including HSP70, HMGB1, and ATP, which are crucial for the maturation of DCs [27]. Our analysis demonstrated that phototherapy using GdCDs and aPD-L1@GdCDs significantly enhanced the release of ATP from the cytoplasm, HMGB1 release from the nuclei, and the expression of HSP70, in contrast to other treatments that had negligible effects (Fig. 3B–D). The increased effectiveness of aPD-L1@GdCDs under laser irradiation is attributed to the targeting of aPD-L1 to cell membrane via its receptor PD-L1. These findings indicate that aPD-L1@GdCDs can effectively induce ICD in cancer cells through phototherapy, promoting the release of DAMPs to provoke a robust pro-inflammatory anti-tumor immune response, thereby facilitating the DCs maturation of and the activation of T lymphocytes.

We evaluated the efficacy of DC maturation in the lower chamber by analyzing the DC-related immune response through flow cytometry, measuring the expression of maturation markers (CD11c+ CD80+ CD86+) (Fig. 3E). The results indicated that treatment of cancer cells with GdCDs and aPD-L1@GdCDs significantly enhanced DC maturation following laser irradiation. In contrast, tumor antigens induced by aPD-L1 and GdCDs had a minimal impact on stimulating DC maturation. These findings highlight the effectiveness of laser-triggered therapy in promoting DC maturation and enhancing the immune response. Quantitative analysis of tumor necrosis factor alpha (TNF-α), a key marker of DC maturation, showed a similar trend of increased cytokine secretion by mature DCs (Fig. 3F). Specifically, the aPD-L1@GdCDs + L treatment exhibited 4.3- or 1.5-fold higher levels of TNF-α compared to the aPD-L1@GdCDs and GdCDs + L groups, respectively. This finding confirms that DAMPs secreted from photo-damaged cancer cells significantly enhance DC maturation. Similarly, this trend of increased immune activation was further supported by the polarization of macrophages from M2 to M1 phenotype, which also served as antigen-presenting cells, confirming the broad immunomodulatory impact of our nanoplatform (Fig. S21).

Given that mature DCs play a crucial role in initiating both innate and adaptive immunity by presenting antigens to T cells, we further evaluated the in vitro proliferation and effector function of T cells in response to these treated DCs. Naïve CD8+ T cells were isolated, stained with carboxyfluorescein succinimidyl ester (CFSE) cell tracer, and co-cultured with the treated DCs for 24 h. Treatment with GdCDs + L or aPD-L1@GdCDs + L significantly increased cytotoxic T cell proliferation compared to other groups, underscoring the effectiveness of phototherapy in promoting DC-to-T cell immune activation (Fig. 3G). Additionally, increased secretion of interferon gamma (IFN-γ), a marker of T cell activation, was observed in these groups, indicating robust T cell activation (Fig. 3H). These findings suggest that phototherapy using aPD-L1@GdCDs offers a promising approach for immunogenic therapy, combining ICB via aPD-L1/PD-L1 interaction with effective CD8+ T cells activation through photo-induced ICD.

To visualize the in vivo distribution of aPD-L1@GdCDs and GdCDs, subcutaneous xenograft tumor models were established by inoculating B16F10 cells in Balb/c mice and 4T1 cells in Balb/c nude mice until the tumors reached volumes of 80–120 mm3. Subsequently, mice were administered aPD-L1@GdCDs intravenously, and their distribution was monitored using FLI, PAI, and MRI. FLI results demonstrated that aPD-L1@GdCDs effectively targeted the tumor sites, with maximum accumulation observed at 4 h post-injection (Fig. 4 A, B). In contrast, GdCDs displayed a broader distribution and peaked at 2 h post-injection (Fig. S22), indicating that aPD-L1 conjugation improved tumor targeting capability and prolonged retention time. This trend was demonstrated by in vivo PAI and MRI imaging results, which supported the utility of aPD-L1@GdCDs as versatile contrast agents for multi-modal cancer imaging (Fig. 4A, C, D). Fluorescence imaging of tumor tissues one day post-injection of FITC-labeled aPD-L1@GdCDs showed strong green fluorescence (aPD-L1) and red fluorescence (GdCDs), confirming effective targeting and retention of aPD-L1@GdCDs within the tumor sites (Fig. 4E). Tumor sites with maximum accumulation of GdCDs and aPD-L1@GdCDs were then exposed to a 650 nm laser for 10 min. Under 0.5 W cm−2 laser irradiation, the local temperature at the tumor site with aPD-L1@GdCDs increased rapidly to 46.8 °C, which is suitable for mild photothermal/photodynamic tumor ablation (Fig. 4F, G). These in vivo imaging results suggest that aPD-L1@GdCDs is a promising theranostic nanoplatform for imaging-guided cancer treatment.

Building on the observed tumor targeting and synergistic therapeutic potential of aPD-L1@GdCDs, we further investigated the anti-tumor effects in live mice models. To evaluate the combined impact of mild phototherapy-induced immune activation and ICB, Balb/c mice bearing B16F10 tumor xenografts were randomly assigned to different treatment groups including blank, laser irradiation (L), GdCDs, aPD-L1, aPD-L1@GdCDs, GdCDs with laser irradiation (GdCDs + L), and aPD-L1@GdCDs with laser irradiation (aPD-L1@GdCDs + L). Treatments were administered on days 0 and 3, with subsequent sacrifice on day 15 for further analysis (Fig. 5 A). Tumor sites were exposed to a 650 nm laser with a power density of 0.5 W cm−2 at peak accumulation, either 2 h or 4 h post-injection. As shown in Fig. 5B, C, tumors in mice treated with aPD-L1@GdCDs + L were nearly completely eradicated, demonstrating the synergistic effects of mild phototherapy and immune activation. In contrast, tumors in mice treated with aPD-L1, aPD-L1@GdCDs, and GdCDs + L experienced only partial elimination, underscoring the limited efficacy of either immunotherapy or mild phototherapy alone. Notably, aPD-L1@GdCDs + L was the most effective in extending survival among tumor-bearing mice compared to the other treatment groups (Fig. 5D). No obvious body weight changes were found for all the groups, indicating the low systemic toxicity during the therapies (Fig. 5E). Pathological analysis of tumor tissues revealed that the aPD-L1@GdCDs + L group exhibited the most substantial tumor destruction, characterized by nuclear shrinkage, pyknosis, and karyorrhexis, while tumors in other groups showed only moderate damage (Fig. 5F). Consistent with the findings from TUNEL staining, aPD-L1@GdCDs + L treatment elicited the highest number of apoptotic cells at the tumor site, while other groups resulted in only limited apoptosis. Additionally, biosafety evaluations of aPD-L1@GdCDs indicated no significant damage to major organs across all treatment groups, suggesting negligible systemic toxicity (Fig. S23). These results were consistently replicated in a 4T1 tumor-bearing mouse model, further confirming that aPD-L1@GdCDs + L could effectively suppress tumor growth with minimal systemic toxicity (Fig. S24).

To elucidate the mechanisms underlying the anti-tumor immunity induced by aPD-L1@GdCDs + L, we evaluated the in vivo immune response by focusing on PD-L1 expression, ICD induction in tumors, immune cell responses within tumors and lymph nodes, and major cytokine secretion in serum. Immunohistochemistry fluorescence staining revealed that PD-L1 expression was upregulated in the GdCDs + L group, likely due to the mild photo-heating effect of GdCDs (Fig. 6 A). In contrast, the aPD-L1@GdCDs + L-treated tumors exhibited a significant reduction in PD-L1 expression, comparable to levels in the aPD-L1@GdCDs and aPD-L1 groups, and markedly lower than those in the control groups, indicating effective PD-L1 inhibition by the PD-L1/aPD-L1 checkpoint blockade. This was supported by studies using a GFP-labeled B16F10 cell-seeded tumor-bearing animal model, where post-treatment analysis of GFP-positive tumor cells via flow cytometry and ELISA confirmed significant suppression of PD-L1 expression induced by photothermal treatment in the aPD-L1@GdCDs + L group. These findings were consistent with the immunostaining results of PD-L1 expression shown in Fig. 6A. These results, illustrated in Fig. S25, which highlighted the profound impact of blockade within the TME. Additionally, phototherapy-induced ICD effect was evaluated by immune fluorescent analysis of HSP70 and HMGB1. The aPD-L1@GdCDs + L treatment showed the highest HSP70 expression due to the photothermal effect and the highest extracellular release of HMGB1 from the nucleus with the associated lowest fluorescence intensity of tumor cells. These results are consistent with in vitro results and demonstrate the effective ICD induction in vivo.

Given that the migration of mature DCs into lymph nodes for antigen presentation is crucial for immunization, we investigated the mechanism behind the superior anti-tumor efficacy by analyzing the responses of DCs and T cells. Tumor tissues were collected and processed into tumor cell suspensions for flow cytometry analysis. Consistent with the increased expression of HSP70 and release of HMGB1, treatment with aPD-L1@GdCDs + L significantly enhanced DC maturation within the lymph nodes. As shown in Fig. 6B, D, the percentage of mature DCs (CD11c+ CD80+ CD86+) in the aPD-L1@GdCDs + L group was 51.5 %, markedly higher than that in the aPD-L1@GdCDs (33.7 %) and GdCDs + L (44.4 %) groups. Furthermore, the populations of CD8+ cytotoxic T cells and CD4+ helper T cells (gated within the CD3+ subset) were evaluated in tumor tissues (Fig. 6C,E). Mild phototherapy with GdCDs + L resulted in a moderate increase in CD8+ T cell frequency (17.5 %), which was 2.9-fold higher than that the 6.1 % observed in the control group, indicating effective activation of ICD through mild phototherapy. Additionally, the aPD-L1@GdCDs + L group exhibited a significant increase in the population of CD8+ T cells (22.0 %) compared to the GdCDs + L group, attributed to the combined effects of ICD activation and ICB of PD-L1/PD-1 interaction by the aPD-L1 component. As shown in Fig. S26, the CD8+/CD4+ cell ratio of all the four therapy groups showed obvious increase compared with the control groups and there was a slight increase of CD8+/CD4+ cell ratio from aPD-L1 group to aPD-L1@GdCDs + L group due to a stronger T cell immune response, potentially reflecting an enhanced ability of the immune system to target and attack tumor cells. CLSM images corroborated that aPD-L1@GdCDs + L markedly enhanced CD8+ T cell infiltration (Fig. 6F). There was a notable rise in serum levels of the cytokines IFN-γ and TNF-α in the aPD-L1@GdCDs + L group, indicating heightened immune cell activity in modulating the immune microenvironment (Fig. 6G, H). These results illustrate that the combination of mild phototherapy with PD-L1 blockade effectively reduces tumor-associated immunosuppression.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo long-term immune memory effect analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The long-term immune memory effects of aPD-L1@GdCDs-mediated photo-immune therapy were assessed in a re-challenged tumor model. In this model, B16F10 cancer cells were intravenously injected into mice that had previously undergone the treatment for 7 days (Fig. 7 A). The anti-recurrence capability was evaluated by measuring the levels of Tem in the spleen and observing tumor metastasis in the liver. A significant increase in Tem was observed in the group treated with aPD-L1@GdCDs + L, indicating a robust immune memory response (Fig. 7B). The liver is one of the most common sites for cancer metastasis, accounting for nearly 25 % of all cases, as the dual blood supply of the liver makes it uniquely susceptible to metastasis [52]. Liver metastasis is particularly challenging due to the liver's unique immune-tolerant environment, which actively diminishes the systemic efficacy of ICB therapies [53]. Here, we further studied the efficacy of our nanoplatform in inhibiting liver metastasis. As shown in Fig. 7C, D, the liver analysis revealed no apparent metastatic lesions in these mice, suggesting that the combined therapy effectively induced a memory response that prevented tumor recurrence. Additionally, mice in the aPD-L1@GdCDs + L group had significantly longer survival time compared to those in the other groups (Fig. 7E). These results indicate that aPD-L1@GdCDs-based photo-immune therapy effectively promotes the formation of T memory cells, leading to a long-term anti-tumor response.

In summary, our research presents a novel aPD-L1@GdCDs theranostic nanoplatform, potentiating combinational photo-immunotherapy. This nanoplatform combines mild PTT, PDT, and ICB via aPD-L1, specifically targeting tumors with elevated PD-L1 expression. Under controlled NIR laser irradiation, the aPD-L1@GdCDs induce immunogenic cell death, triggering a strong immune response characterized by cytokine secretion and DC maturation. This process enhances T cell activation and promotes T cell infiltration into the TME. Consequently, the efficacy of the PD-1/PD-L1 blockade is significantly improved, effectively reversing tumor-associated immunosuppression and boosting cytotoxic anti-tumor activity. As expected, aPD-L1@GdCDs has demonstrated superior performance in both in vitro and in vivo studies, showing marked improvement in tumor ablation, anti-metastasis, and overall ICB effectiveness. Compared to previous photoimmunotherapy paradigms that often involve complex functional components and multi-step synthesis procedures with organic solvents, our work inspires a facile method by simply integrating CDs with aPD-L1 [39,54,55]. The doping of Gd3+ into CDs significantly improves multi-modal imaging capabilities, including FLI, PAI, and MRI. This enhancement is crucial for accurate, real-time tracking of therapeutic delivery and tumor response. Notably, the superior r1 (25.3 mM−1 s−1) of our work greatly boosts the quality of T1-weighted MRI, offering clear and accurate imaging that surpasses reported Gd3+-incorporated CDs by Shi et al. (6.38 mM−1 s−1) [56], Du et al. (6.45 mM−1 s−1) [57], Shu et al. (11.16 mM−1 s−1) [58], and Jiao et al. (13.4 mM−1 s−1) [59]. Looking ahead, our efforts will concentrate on optimizing and scaling this nanoplatform for assessing its effectiveness across various cancer types.
